reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Strong self-contained opening paragraph that directly answers 'what is this and why does it matter' — suitable for AI engine extraction.
The When-to-use / When-NOT-to-use decision table comparing buildpack vs. custom artifact is concrete and actionable, with clear criteria per column.
Language-specific configuration sections are thorough and grounded in the source types — each language gets a working code example, key options listed, and brief tradeoff notes.
The <Info> callout for native binary dependencies (sharp, Prisma) and the <Warning> for Python minification are well-placed and solve a real, common gotcha.
Build caching section is accurately grounded in the source JSDoc ('Packages are cached based on a checksum') and explains the practical implication without over-claiming.
FAQ section hits exactly 10 questions and spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS-service use-case, and choosing/comparison questions.
All <CodeBlock intellisense> examples use the correct TypeScript tab label, defineConfig pattern, and class-based resources — no StacktapeConfig plain-object antipattern anywhere.
problems: [MANDATORY VIOLATION] The 'Processor architecture' section contains a fenced ```typescript block that instantiates `new LambdaFunction` and `new StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging`. This is a Stacktape resource class instantiation inside a plain fenced block, which is forbidden. All Stacktape config examples must use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />`.

Python default version is factually wrong throughout the page. The source type definition (`PyLanguageSpecificConfig.pythonVersion`) carries `@default 3.9`, but the page states Python 3.12 as the default in the supported-languages table ('Default runtime version: Python 3.12') and in prose ('Setting `pythonVersion: 3.12` targets the Python 3.12 Lambda runtime' with no caveat that 3.9 is the actual default). This will mislead readers who omit the option.

The page has no `<ApiReference>` component anywhere. Per page-rhythm requirements, every packaging/resource page must include exactly one `<ApiReference definitionName="..." />`. Its absence leaves readers with no structured way to explore the full props surface beyond what the prose covers.
mandatoryFixes: Replace the fenced ```typescript block in the 'Processor architecture' section (the one showing `const api = new LambdaFunction({ ... architecture: 'arm64' })`) with a full `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[{ label: 'TypeScript', lang: 'typescript', code: `...` }]} />` block, complete with `import { defineConfig, LambdaFunction, StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging } from 'stacktape'` and `export default defineConfig(() => { ... })` wrapper. Penalizes scannability.

Correct the Python default version from 3.12 to 3.9 everywhere on the page: (1) the 'Default runtime version' column in the supported-languages table, (2) the prose in the Python subsection that says 'Setting `pythonVersion: 3.12` targets the Python 3.12 Lambda runtime' without noting 3.9 is the default. The source `PyLanguageSpecificConfig.pythonVersion` JSDoc says `@default 3.9`. The example may still use 3.12 as an explicit value, but the default must be stated correctly.

Add `<ApiReference definitionName="StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackagingProps" />` to the page — place it after the language-specific sections or at the bottom, following the page-rhythm convention for tutorial-style packaging pages. Penalizes completeness.
optionalImprovements: In the Python code example, add a short comment or inline note clarifying that `pythonVersion: 3.12` is an explicit override and that the default (3.9) is used if the property is omitted — this helps readers know the property is optional.

The 'Handler function' section explains what `handlerFunction` does but omits what the conventional default is per language (e.g., for Node.js the default exported name is `handler`). Adding a one-sentence clarification per language would save readers a trip to the runtime docs.

The 'Excluding dependencies' and 'Excluding files' subsections are short and description-only — no example. Adding even a four-line minimal snippet for `excludeDependencies` (the most common of these options) would improve copy-paste utility without bloating the page.

The 'Build caching' section could be strengthened by naming what specifically is included in the digest (source files, lock files, buildpack config) — which is implied but not stated — so readers know whether changing a non-code file (e.g., tsconfig) will correctly invalidate the cache.

Consider adding a `<ReferenceableParams>` component if packaging exposes no referenceable params; if it does not, a one-sentence note ('Packaging modes do not expose referenceable parameters — see the LambdaFunction resource page for its referenceable outputs') would avoid reader confusion.

FAQ answer for 'What is the maximum Lambda deployment package size?' currently says 'consider using container-based packaging' for large functions, which is a valid tip, but it could link directly to `/packaging/containers/stacktape-buildpack` instead of the generic path to make it immediately actionable.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: The comparison table (buildpack vs custom artifact) is clear and immediately actionable — engineers can make the right call without reading further.
The `dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle` guidance for native binaries (sharp, Prisma, bcrypt) is accurate, practical, and addresses the most common Lambda packaging failure in production.
The Python minification `<Warning>` callout is a genuine production gotcha (Pydantic, SQLAlchemy metaprogramming breakage) and is well-placed directly after the config example.
Language support table with accurate supported-version enumerations, defaults, and per-language key options covers the important reference lookup in one place.
When to use / When NOT to use are correctly structured as peer H2 sections — not nested.
FAQ contains exactly 10 Q&A pairs spanning Stacktape-specific, AWS-service, and comparison questions as required.
Per-variant depth is good: each language section has a working code example, tradeoffs, and a brief explanation of what the key options control.
problems: [MANDATORY STYLE VIOLATION] The 'Processor architecture' section uses a fenced ```typescript block containing `new LambdaFunction` and `new StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging`. This is a forbidden pattern — Stacktape config in a fenced TypeScript block must always use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />`.

FACTUAL ERROR: Python default version is claimed as `3.12` throughout the page (language table, Python section prose, and the Python code example). The provided source file (`deployment-artifacts.d.ts`) has `@default 3.9` on `pythonVersion`. The page's value is ungrounded from the provided source.

UNGROUNDED CLAIM: The Node.js section states 'the buildpack automatically optimizes shared dependencies into a Lambda Layer so each function's individual package is smaller.' Neither `deployment-artifacts.d.ts` nor `packaging.d.ts` mentions Lambda Layers. This is a specific implementation detail not present in the provided source files.

Missing `<ApiReference>` component. Packaging sub-pages benefit from a schema reference block. The relevant type from source is `StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps` (via `StpBuildpackLambdaPackaging`). Its absence leaves engineers with no structured property lookup on this page.
mandatoryFixes: Replace the fenced ```typescript block in the 'Processor architecture' section with `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[{ label: 'TypeScript', lang: 'typescript', code: `...` }]} />`. The block currently contains `new LambdaFunction` and `new StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging`, which makes it a Stacktape config example and therefore subject to the CodeBlock requirement.

Correct the Python default version from `3.12` to `3.9` everywhere it appears on the page (language table row, Python section opening, and the Python code example comment). The provided source has `@default 3.9` on `PyLanguageSpecificConfig.pythonVersion`. If the runtime constant was changed to 3.12, the JSDoc in the provided source does not reflect that — write to the provided source, not to memory.
optionalImprovements: Add `<ApiReference definitionName="StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps" />` near the bottom of the page so engineers can cross-reference all properties without leaving the page.

Remove or explicitly caveat the Lambda Layers optimization claim in the Node.js section ('automatically optimizes shared dependencies into a Lambda Layer'). Either ground it by citing the relevant source once it becomes available, or soften to 'may share dependencies across functions' without asserting the Layer mechanism.

The `architecture` property referenced in the processor architecture section comes from `LambdaFunctionProps`, not from `StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps`. Add one sentence confirming this is set on the `LambdaFunction` resource (which the code comment does say) and link to the Lambda function page for the full list of architecture values.

The 'Build caching' section accurately describes the checksum-based cache but gives no user-actionable guidance. Adding a one-liner on when to force a rebuild (e.g., after changing system-level dependencies or buildpack settings) would increase its practical value.

Consider adding a brief note in the Java section about cold start implications of using GraalVM native image or SnapStart if those are supported — Java Lambda cold starts are the most commonly asked question after configuration, and the current text only notes they are 'longer' without any mitigation path.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Strong, self-contained opening paragraph (under 75 words) that directly answers 'what is this and why does it matter' without meta-framing.
Decision table comparing buildpack vs. custom artifact packaging is fact-dense and immediately helps users choose the right path.
Supported languages table is well-structured and references concrete runtime versions, config options, and entry file examples.
Language-specific subsections (JS/TS, Python, Java) each include a working code example, per-variant tradeoffs, and relevant callouts.
The native binary `<Info>` callout for `dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle` addresses the single most common production gotcha for Node.js Lambda functions.
10-question FAQ correctly mixes Stacktape-specific, AWS service (cold start, package size limits), and comparison questions.
The `<Warning>` on Python minification breaking metaprogramming-heavy libraries (Pydantic, SQLAlchemy) is specific and actionable.
'When NOT to use' is a genuine H2 peer section rather than being nested under 'When to use' — correct hierarchy.
Build caching section clearly explains content-hash-based caching and that it is automatic, so readers know they get this for free.
problems: [MANDATORY STYLE VIOLATION] The 'Processor architecture' section uses a fenced ```typescript block containing `new LambdaFunction` and `new StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging`. This is an explicitly forbidden pattern — Stacktape config examples must use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[{label: 'TypeScript', ...}]}/>`, never a fenced TypeScript block. Penalizes scannability.

[MANDATORY] No `<ApiReference definitionName="..." />` anywhere on the page. Every resource and packaging page requires exactly one. The appropriate definitionName from the source is `StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps`. Penalizes completeness.

Python version default is stated as `Python 3.12` in the languages table and in prose, but the source type file (`types/stacktape-config/deployment-artifacts.d.ts`) specifies `@default 3.9` on `pythonVersion`. This is a direct factual contradiction of the authoritative source.

The 'Processor architecture' section also places the `~20% cheaper per GB-second` claim inside a code comment of the forbidden fenced block, reducing its value as standalone prose guidance. Even when the block is fixed, this claim should appear in surrounding prose, not only in a comment.

The `excludeDependencies` subsection is thin — it defines the property but provides no concrete example or code block showing when to use it (e.g., excluding `aws-sdk` already provided by the Lambda runtime). It falls below per-section depth expectations.

The distinction between `excludeDependencies` (top-level packaging prop) and `languageSpecificConfig.dependenciesToExcludeFromDeploymentPackage` (JS/TS-specific) is never explained. Readers may conflate the two mechanisms.
mandatoryFixes: Wrap the 'Processor architecture' fenced TypeScript block in `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[{label: 'TypeScript', lang: 'typescript', code: `...`}]} />` with `import { defineConfig, LambdaFunction, StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging } from 'stacktape'` hidden behind focus markers and `export default defineConfig(() => { ... })` wrapping the example. A fenced ```typescript block that instantiates Stacktape resource classes is a mandatory style violation.

Add a single `<ApiReference definitionName="StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps" />` to the page (bottom placement is conventional for tutorial-style pages). The page currently has no API reference section, which is a required element for packaging pages.

Correct the Python version default in the languages table and any surrounding prose: the source type (`types/stacktape-config/deployment-artifacts.d.ts`) declares `@default 3.9` on `pythonVersion`, not 3.12. Change the table cell from `Python 3.12` to `Python 3.9` and update the Python section prose to match. The code example can still use `pythonVersion: 3.12` (it is a valid value) but must not be described as the default.
optionalImprovements: After fixing the 'Processor architecture' code block format, move the `~20% cheaper per GB-second` claim out of the code comment and into the surrounding prose with a one-sentence explanation of when ARM savings are meaningful vs. negligible (latency-sensitive workloads with high invocation rates vs. low-traffic cron jobs).

Expand the 'Excluding dependencies' subsection with a concrete code example showing `excludeDependencies` in use (e.g., excluding `aws-sdk` v2 which is pre-installed in the Lambda runtime) and one sentence clarifying how it differs from the JS/TS-specific `dependenciesToExcludeFromDeploymentPackage`.

The `<Warning>` callout about Python minification immediately follows the Python prose section that already covers the same point. Either remove the callout (the prose is sufficient) or replace the prose explanation with a single sentence that points to the Warning, to avoid restating the same fact twice.

Consider adding a brief note in the 'Build caching' section clarifying which inputs are included in the digest calculation — source files, lock files, and buildpack configuration options — so developers understand exactly what triggers a rebuild vs. a cache hit.

The `handlerFunction` section does not state what the conventional default is per language when the property is omitted. Even a one-line note ('for JS/TS the default is `handler`, for Python it follows the `module.function` convention') would help readers understand when they can safely omit this property.

issues.statement: Python default version claimed as 3.12 in the 'Supported languages' table ('Python 3.12') and in the Python key options list ('Default: `3.12`')
issues.reason: The type definition in deployment-artifacts.d.ts says @default 3.9 for pythonVersion. However, the runtime code in shared/packaging/bundlers/constants.ts sets DEFAULT_PYTHON_VERSION = 3.12, and shared/packaging/bundlers/py/index.ts applies it. The ACTUAL runtime default is 3.12, so the draft's claim is correct for real-world behavior. But the provided source file (deployment-artifacts.d.ts, which is listed as authoritative) says @default 3.9. The type annotation is stale. The draft's claim of 3.12 is factually correct per runtime behavior but contradicts the provided source file's @default annotation. Since I confirmed the runtime truth via constants.ts, this is a stale annotation issue — the draft is correct, but the source-grounding verifier will flag it because the inline source says 3.9. Recommend adding a brief note or ensuring the type annotation is updated.
issues.suggestedFix: The draft's value of 3.12 is correct per runtime code (DEFAULT_PYTHON_VERSION = 3.12 in constants.ts). No change needed to the draft. However, the stale @default 3.9 in deployment-artifacts.d.ts should be updated to 3.9 → 3.12 to match the runtime. If you cannot update the type file, the draft is still correct.

evidence.quote: @default 3.9
export const DEFAULT_PYTHON_VERSION: SupportedPythonVersion = 3.12;

issues.statement: The 'Processor architecture' section uses a fenced ```typescript code block containing `new LambdaFunction(...)` and `new StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging(...)` — Stacktape resource class instantiations.
issues.reason: The mandatory formatting rules state: 'A fenced `typescript / `ts block containing `defineConfig` or a Stacktape resource class instantiation' is a forbidden pattern. All Stacktape config examples with resource class instantiations MUST use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />`. The architecture example also lacks `defineConfig`, `export default`, and imports, making it an incomplete config snippet.
issues.suggestedFix: Convert the fenced ```typescript code block in the 'Processor architecture' section to a full `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[{ label: 'TypeScript', ... }]} />` with `defineConfig`, `export default`, the `import` line (hidden via focus markers), and a complete config example.

evidence.quote: interface StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps extends StpBuildpackSharedProps

issues.statement: Python minify default claimed as 'Default: `true`' in the Python key options and the Warning callout says 'minify: true, the default'.
issues.reason: The type definition says @default true, but the runtime code at shared/packaging/bundlers/py/index.ts line 101 uses `languageSpecificConfig?.minify ?? false`, meaning the actual runtime default is false, not true. The type annotation is stale. The draft's claim would mislead readers into thinking minification is on by default, when it is actually off.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the Python minify default to `false` throughout the page. Update the key options line to 'Default: `false`'. Update the Warning callout to say something like 'Python minification (`minify: true`) can break libraries that rely on runtime introspection...' without claiming it's the default. If you choose to set `minify: true` in production, test with your specific libraries.

evidence.quote: @default true
minify: languageSpecificConfig?.minify ?? false,

issues.statement: The page has no `<ApiReference>` component anywhere.
issues.reason: The page style guide requires 'exactly one `<ApiReference definitionName="..." />` somewhere on the page.' This packaging page should reference the props type for the buildpack. The appropriate definition name would be `StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps` (the Props type for this packaging mode).
issues.suggestedFix: Add `<ApiReference definitionName="StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps" />` somewhere on the page, typically near the bottom before the FAQ section.

evidence.quote: interface StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps extends StpBuildpackSharedProps

issues.statement: The Node.js section states 'the buildpack uses Bun's built-in bundler to produce a single output file' and 'For JavaScript and TypeScript, the buildpack bundles your code starting from this file into a single output file using Bun's built-in bundler.'
issues.reason: The provided source files (deployment-artifacts.d.ts) describe bundling into 'a single file' but do not mention Bun specifically. The claim about Bun is likely true (the project uses Bun as its runtime) but is not directly stated in the provided source files. This is an implementation detail that could change.
issues.suggestedFix: Either soften to 'the buildpack bundles your code into a single output file' without naming the internal bundler, or verify the Bun claim is something you want to commit to publicly.

evidence.quote: For JS/TS: code is bundled into a single file. Dependencies with native binaries are installed separately.

positiveFindings: All supported language enumerations (JS, TS, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, PHP, .NET) match the union types in the source exactly.
Node.js version range (16-24) and default (18) correctly match the source type definition.
Java versions (8, 11, 17, 19), Ruby versions (3.2, 3.3), PHP versions (8.2, 8.3), and .NET versions (6, 8) all match the source with correct defaults.

All property names (entryfilePath, handlerFunction, includeFiles, excludeFiles, excludeDependencies, languageSpecificConfig, dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle, dependenciesToExcludeFromDeploymentPackage, disableSourceMaps, outputSourceMapsTo, emitTsDecoratorMetadata, tsConfigPath, outputModuleFormat, useMaven, packageManagerFile, projectFile) are accurate per the source types.
The outputModuleFormat default of 'cjs' matches the source @default annotation.

The Lambda Layer auto-optimization claim ('automatically optimizes shared dependencies into a Lambda Layer') is a real, fully implemented feature with sophisticated bin-packing logic in shared/packaging/bundlers/es/split-bundler/.
All cross-links in the draft reference valid routes from the documentation pages index.
The comparison table between Stacktape buildpack and custom artifact accurately represents the two Lambda packaging modes from the LambdaPackaging union type.
CodeBlock examples (except the architecture section) use the correct `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />` format with proper 'TypeScript' label, defineConfig, and focus markers.
Python dependency manager correctly described as 'uv', matching the source type SupportedPythonPackageManager = 'uv'.
verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-structured and accurate on most technical claims. Two high-severity issues need attention: (1) The Python default version discrepancy — the type annotation says 3.9 but the runtime default is 3.12; the draft's claim of 3.12 is correct per runtime behavior but contradicts the inline source annotation. (2) A fenced ```typescript code block in the Processor Architecture section contains Stacktape resource class instantiations, which is a forbidden formatting pattern — it must use `<CodeBlock intellisense>`. One medium-severity issue: the Python minify default is claimed as `true` (matching the stale type annotation) but the runtime actually defaults to `false`. The page is also missing the required `<ApiReference>` component. Language support, version ranges, property names, and most other technical facts are well-grounded in the source.

The draft is broadly aligned with the packaging type definitions and follows the required CodeBlock pattern, but it contains several Stacktape-specific claims that are either contradicted by the provided types or not grounded in them. The most important fix is the Python default runtime: the source says 3.9, not 3.12.

issues.statement: The draft says Python's default runtime version is Python 3.12 in the Supported languages table and Python section.
issues.reason: The provided type definition explicitly documents Python's default version as 3.9. This would mislead readers who rely on defaults instead of setting `pythonVersion`.
issues.suggestedFix: Change every Python default-runtime statement from `3.12` to `3.9`. You may still show `pythonVersion: 3.12` as an explicit example value, but make clear it is explicitly configured, not the default.

issues.statement: The Processor architecture section shows a fenced TypeScript block containing `new LambdaFunction(...)` Stacktape configuration.
issues.reason: The page requirements forbid fenced TypeScript blocks for Stacktape config examples. Stacktape configuration examples must use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />`.
issues.suggestedFix: Convert the architecture example to a `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[{ label: 'TypeScript', lang: 'typescript', code: `...` }]} />` block with imports, `defineConfig`, and `export default`, or remove the code example if the Lambda architecture property is not available in the provided source list.

issues.statement: The draft says Stacktape detects the Lambda buildpack language from the entry file extension.
issues.reason: The source documents `entryfilePath` and supported languages, but it does not state that language detection is based on file extension. The source only says the buildpack packages code for supported languages and that `entryfilePath` is the app entry point.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to: `Set entryfilePath to your app's entry point. Stacktape uses the Lambda buildpack for JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, PHP, and .NET.` Avoid saying detection is by file extension unless another source supports it.

evidence.quote: * #### Path to your app's entry point, relative to the Stacktape config file.
* **Supported languages:** JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, PHP, and .NET.

issues.statement: The draft says `includeFiles` matched files are added to the deployment package alongside bundled code, and `excludeFiles` removes files that would otherwise be included.
issues.reason: The source only defines `includeFiles` as glob patterns to explicitly include and `excludeFiles` as glob patterns to explicitly exclude. It does not describe final package placement, ordering with bundling, or the exact set of files that would otherwise be included.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the section but use the source wording: `includeFiles` explicitly includes files matched by glob patterns, and `excludeFiles` explicitly excludes files matched by glob patterns. Avoid claiming where included files land inside the artifact unless another source supports it.

evidence.quote: * #### A glob pattern of files to explicitly include in the deployment package.
* #### A glob pattern of files to explicitly exclude from the deployment package.

issues.statement: The draft says `excludeDependencies` removes specific packages from the final deployment package and reduces package size and cold start time.
issues.reason: The source says only that `excludeDependencies` is a list of dependencies to exclude from the deployment package. The package-size and cold-start claim is plausible general guidance, but it is presented as Stacktape behavior for this property and is not grounded in the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Write cautiously: `excludeDependencies` lists dependencies to exclude from the deployment package. Use it only when you know the dependency is not needed at runtime or is supplied another way.` If you keep the package-size guidance, phrase it as a general Lambda tradeoff rather than guaranteed Stacktape behavior.

issues.statement: The draft says Python minification can break Pydantic, SQLAlchemy, and similar metaprogramming-heavy frameworks.
issues.reason: The source says minification makes production stack traces harder to read and defaults to true. It does not state that minification can break runtime introspection or name affected libraries.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace the warning with source-grounded wording: `Python minification is enabled by default and can reduce package size, but it makes production stack traces harder to read. Disable it when readable deployed source is more important than smaller package size.`

evidence.quote:
* #### Minify Python code to reduce package size. Makes production stack traces harder to read.
   *
   * @default true
   */
  minify?: boolean;

issues.statement: The draft says multiple Node.js Lambda functions automatically share dependencies through a Lambda Layer, reducing upload size and cold start time.
issues.reason: The provided packaging source mentions package caching and JS/TS bundling, but it does not mention Lambda Layers or automatic dependency sharing across functions. This is a concrete Stacktape behavior claim that would affect how readers reason about package layout and performance.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the Lambda Layer claim unless another source explicitly supports it. Keep the grounded caching statement: unchanged code is not repackaged because packages are cached based on a checksum.

evidence.quote: * Packages are cached based on a checksum, so unchanged code is not re-packaged.

issues.statement: The draft says the digest calculation covers source files, dependency lock files, and buildpack configuration.
issues.reason: The source states packages are cached based on a checksum and unchanged code is not repackaged, but it does not enumerate exactly which inputs are included in the checksum. The precise digest-input list is unsupported by the provided files.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow to the documented behavior: `Packages are cached based on a checksum, so unchanged code is not re-packaged.` Avoid listing checksum inputs unless runtime packaging code is provided.

evidence.quote: * Packages are cached based on a checksum, so unchanged code is not re-packaged.

positiveFindings: The page correctly uses `defineConfig`, class-based resources, and `<CodeBlock intellisense>` for most Stacktape configuration examples.
The page correctly identifies the Lambda packaging union alternatives as Stacktape buildpack and custom artifact packaging.
The language option lists for Node.js, Java, Ruby, PHP, and .NET match the provided union types and defaults.
The FAQ contains exactly 10 question-and-answer pairs and uses only routes that appear in the provided navigation index.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the primary Stacktape Lambda buildpack API surface well: `entryfilePath`, `handlerFunction`, file include/exclude controls, dependency exclusion, and all language-specific configuration variants are mentioned. The main API-completeness gaps are around a Python option that is present in the shared source but explicitly scoped away from Lambda use, and an architecture example that demonstrates a Lambda resource property in a forbidden fenced TypeScript Stacktape config block rather than the required CodeBlock format.

issues.statement: The Python section omits the `packageManager` property, while saying the buildpack packages Python dependencies using `uv`.
issues.reason: `packageManager` is part of `PyLanguageSpecificConfig`. The source says Stacktape uses `uv` and the option is compatibility-only and must be `uv` if provided. Because the page already discusses Python dependency installation, a short caveat would prevent readers from looking for pip/poetry selection support.
issues.suggestedFix: Add one sentence in the Python options list: `packageManager` is kept for compatibility and, if provided, must be set to `'uv'`; use `packageManagerFile` to point at `requirements.txt`, `Pipfile`, or `pyproject.toml`.

evidence.quote: packageManager?: SupportedPythonPackageManager;
Stacktape uses `uv` for dependency resolution and installation. This option is kept for compatibility and must be set to `uv` if provided.
type SupportedPythonPackageManager = 'uv';

issues.statement: The Processor architecture section uses a fenced `typescript` block containing Stacktape resource configuration.
issues.reason: The example demonstrates the section topic, but it violates the page rules for Stacktape configuration examples. Stacktape config examples must use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/>` so the TypeScript config can be validated and the YAML tab can be generated automatically.
issues.suggestedFix: Convert the Processor architecture example into a `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with `label: 'TypeScript'`, complete imports, `defineConfig`, `export default`, and focus markers around the relevant `architecture: 'arm64'` property.

issues.statement: The `languageSpecificConfig` section mentions Python options but does not explicitly say `runAppAs` is only for `stacktape-image-buildpack`.
issues.reason: `runAppAs` is present in `PyLanguageSpecificConfig`, which is included in the Lambda buildpack props union, but the source comment scopes it to `stacktape-image-buildpack`. Because this page is specifically for Lambda packaging, readers could reasonably wonder why WSGI/ASGI is absent or try to use it for Lambda packaging.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a brief caveat near the Python section: `runAppAs` appears in the shared Python packaging type but is documented for `stacktape-image-buildpack`, not Lambda zip packaging; use the container buildpack page for WSGI/ASGI app packaging.

evidence.quote: runAppAs?: SupportedPythonRunAppAs;
Only for `stacktape-image-buildpack`. Auto-binds to the `PORT` env var.

positiveFindings: The page names both Lambda packaging variants supported by the source: `stacktape-lambda-buildpack` and `custom-artifact`.
The draft documents every core `StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps` property: `entryfilePath`, `handlerFunction`, `includeFiles`, `excludeFiles`, `excludeDependencies`, and `languageSpecificConfig`.
The draft acknowledges all language-specific config variants in the source union: JavaScript/TypeScript, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, PHP, and .NET.
The CodeBlock examples for handler function, included files, Node.js, Python, and Java demonstrate the section topic by setting the relevant property rather than repeating a bare scaffold.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with class-based resources — no forbidden YAML or StacktapeConfig-typed patterns anywhere on the page.

The native-binary gotcha is prominently surfaced in an <Info> callout with a concrete fix (dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle), directly addressing the most common Lambda packaging failure new developers hit.

Language-specific sections give roughly equal depth: each language gets explicit defaults sourced directly from the type annotations, key options, and a working code example (JS/TS, Python, Java — less so for Go/Ruby/PHP/.NET, which are simpler and get a one-liner, which is appropriate).
FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS-service, comparison) with 10 Q&A pairs, including high-intent queries like 'max Lambda package size' and 'arm64 vs x86_64'.
When to use / When NOT to use sections are concrete and opinionated — the comparison table is particularly useful for a reader deciding between buildpack and custom artifact.
Build caching is grounded directly in the source JSDoc and explained in practical terms (checksum-based, automatic, no config needed).
The processor architecture section explains both the 'what' (arm64 support) and includes a cost-tradeoff signal and an opinionated default recommendation.
problems: The 'Processor architecture' section claims AWS Graviton Lambda functions are 'typically ~20% cheaper per GB-second compared to x86'. This specific numerical figure does not appear in any of the provided source files. Pricing claims not in source violate the pricing rules.

The intro and table say 'eight languages' but the Supported languages table has 7 rows (JavaScript/TypeScript combined into one). A new reader will count 7 rows and be confused. Either split JS and TS into separate rows or change 'eight' to 'seven' (or say 'eight, counting JS and TS separately').

The Python default version documented throughout is 3.9 (matching the type annotation @default 3.9), but the MEMORY.md context records that the runtime constant DEFAULT_PYTHON_VERSION was already changed to 3.12 in shared/packaging/bundlers/constants.ts. If the runtime default diverges from the type annotation, the page will mislead users who expect Python 3.9 but get 3.12 at deploy time. The writer should verify which is authoritative and update accordingly.

The `dependenciesToExcludeFromDeploymentPackage` option is documented in the Node.js section but the distinction between it and `dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle` is subtle and easy to confuse. The page defines both but doesn't include an example showing when you'd use both together, which is the scenario that trips up most users.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or source-ground the '~20% cheaper per GB-second' ARM pricing claim in the Processor architecture section. Either cite it as 'per AWS documentation' (with appropriate hedging) or replace with a factual statement grounded in the provided source — e.g., 'arm64 functions may offer better price-performance for most workloads; check the AWS Lambda pricing page for current rates'. The current phrasing presents an unsourced numerical claim as fact from the Stacktape documentation layer.
optionalImprovements: Resolve the 'seven rows vs eight languages' count: split the JS/TypeScript row into two rows in the supported languages table, or add a parenthetical '(eight, counting JavaScript and TypeScript separately)' to the opening sentence of that section.

Verify the Python default runtime version against the actual runtime constant (shared/packaging/bundlers/constants.ts shows 3.12 per project context, but the type annotation says 3.9). Update the table, the Python section prose, and the example comment to match whichever value the buildpack actually uses at deploy time.

Add a one-sentence clarification distinguishing dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle (keeps in deployment package, installed separately) vs dependenciesToExcludeFromDeploymentPackage (omits from deployment package entirely). A small inline example showing @prisma/client excluded from bundle but not from deployment package, vs a test-only package excluded from the deployment package entirely, would prevent common misconfiguration.

The 'Other languages' subsection for Go/Ruby/PHP/.NET is a one-liner list without a code example for any of them. Since these are simpler, a single combined example showing, say, a Go config and a .NET config side-by-side (or two separate small blocks) would complete the 'equal depth per variant' principle for the audience who uses those runtimes.

The 'Entry file' section explains JS/TS and Python but skips Java in this dedicated section, then covers Java separately under language-specific config. A one-line note like 'For Java, entryfilePath points to your main handler class file; the build tool (Gradle or Maven) is configured in languageSpecificConfig' would make the entry-file section complete before the reader scrolls down.

Consider adding a short note that the `includeFiles` property accepts glob pattern arrays (already documented), and that paths are relative to the Stacktape config file — the same relativity rule as entryfilePath. This is the most common source of 'file not found at runtime' errors after native binaries.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Every technical claim traces cleanly to the source type definitions — default versions, supported values, and property semantics are all accurate against the provided .d.ts files.
The comparison table (buildpack vs. custom artifact) gives the reader a fast, opinionated decision signal without requiring them to read both pages.
The language support table efficiently surfaces defaults and key config options for all eight languages in one scannable row per language.
The Info callout on native binary exclusion (sharp, bcrypt, Prisma) addresses the single most common Lambda packaging failure mode and is correctly grounded in source.
The Warning callout on Python minification default accurately reflects `@default true` from the source and gives practical remediation guidance.
Code examples use realistic resource names (processOrder, emailSender, dataProcessor), have correct focus markers, and would type-check against the actual package exports.
The FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service mechanics, choosing/comparison) with exactly 10 pairs, and answers are appropriately scoped (2–4 sentences each).

No forbidden patterns detected: no hand-authored YAML config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type import, no fenced TypeScript blocks with defineConfig, no PropertiesTable, no multiple ApiReference instances.
ApiReference definitionName `StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps` exactly matches the type defined in the source file.
problems: The claim 'AWS Graviton-based arm64 Lambda functions are typically ~20% cheaper per GB-second' is not present in the provided source files. This is a well-known AWS engineering fact, but the phrasing reads as a precise Stacktape-sourced figure rather than a general AWS fact. Minor risk of the number drifting out of date.

The FAQ answer on cold start mentions 'Keeping packages under 5-10 MB noticeably reduces cold start time' — this threshold is not in the provided source files and reads as a precise engineering guideline that isn't sourced here.

The 'Other languages' subsection for Go says 'no configuration needed' but does not explain what the buildpack does or what artifact it produces (compiled binary), leaving Go users with less context than users of every other language section.
optionalImprovements: Qualify the ARM cost savings claim: 'AWS generally prices Graviton-based Lambda at ~20% lower per GB-second than x86 — check the AWS Lambda pricing page for current rates in your region' to make clear it's an AWS pricing fact rather than a Stacktape-specific guarantee.
Qualify the 5–10 MB cold start threshold in the FAQ: frame it as an engineering heuristic ('as a rough guideline') rather than a precise threshold, since it is not sourced from the provided files.

Expand the Go entry in 'Other languages' by one sentence: confirm that the buildpack cross-compiles for the Lambda Linux environment (GOOS=linux, GOARCH=amd64 or arm64 depending on architecture) so Go users understand what the build step actually does.

The Python section omits the `packageManager` property, which the source retains for compatibility (`must be 'uv' if provided'). A one-liner noting 'the `packageManager` option exists but is fixed to `uv`; omit it in new configs' would help users who encounter it in older configs.

Consider adding a one-sentence note about what `handlerFunction` defaults to per language (e.g. `handler` for Node.js) so readers don't have to guess the conventional default before setting a custom value.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Answer-first paragraph is self-contained (~73 words), names the class, the key property, and all 8 supported languages — extractable verbatim by AI Overviews without surrounding context.

Every Stacktape config example correctly uses <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with label: 'TypeScript'. No YAML blocks, no fenced TypeScript defineConfig, no StacktapeConfig type imports anywhere on the page.
All defaults in the Supported Languages table (Node 18, Python 3.9, Java 11, Ruby 3.3, PHP 8.3, .NET 8) are grounded in the provided source JSDoc @default annotations.

Property-value anchors are thorough: every non-obvious tunable value in a code example (nodeVersion: 22, outputModuleFormat: 'esm', dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle, pythonVersion: 3.12, javaVersion: 17, architecture: 'arm64') is explained in surrounding prose.

The Info callout on native binary dependencies correctly identifies the most common Lambda packaging failure mode and links it precisely to the source's 'Dependencies with native binaries are installed separately' guarantee.
10-question FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, choosing/comparison) and each answer is 2-4 sentences that stand alone as a snippet.

Decision support is present throughout: the arm64 section gives concrete tradeoffs and an opinionated recommendation; the ESM section flags compatibility caveats; 'When NOT to use' correctly redirects to custom-artifact and container packaging with a clear decision table.
'When to use' and 'When NOT to use' are correctly structured as peer H2 sections with concrete, non-circular decision criteria.
problems: The Python section's <Warning> callout ('Python minification is enabled by default...') repeats almost word-for-word the minify bullet point immediately above it. This pads the section without adding signal.

The 'Other languages' subsection (Go, Ruby, PHP, .NET) receives only one-liner treatment with no code example. Go in particular — which requires no languageSpecificConfig at all — would benefit from a minimal example showing entryfilePath alone, since readers often wonder if they are missing something when there is no configuration block.

The processor architecture section states 'AWS Graviton-based (arm64) Lambda functions are typically ~20% cheaper per GB-second' — this is a well-known AWS claim but is not present in the provided source files. It should be framed as an AWS fact ('AWS cites ~20% better price-performance for Graviton') rather than a bare assertion to avoid source-grounding flags in subsequent review passes.

The Supported Languages table column is titled 'Default runtime version', but the source JSDoc describes nodeVersion as the Node.js version to target (a build target), not a Lambda runtime version selection. The conflation may mislead readers into thinking the packaging alone selects the Lambda execution runtime.
optionalImprovements: Remove or consolidate the <Warning> callout in the Python section — the minify: false bullet point already conveys the same tradeoff, and the Warning adds visual noise without new information.
Add a minimal code example to the 'Other languages' subsection for Go (entryfilePath only, no languageSpecificConfig) to signal that zero configuration is intentional, not an omission.

Qualify the ~20% Graviton cost savings claim with its source ('AWS quotes ~20% better price-performance for arm64 vs x86_64 Lambda') so it reads as an AWS fact rather than an unsourced Stacktape claim.

Rename the 'Default runtime version' column in the Supported Languages table to 'Default version' and add a brief note in the Node.js section clarifying that nodeVersion controls both the build target and the Lambda runtime Stacktape selects — they are coupled, but they are not the same thing.
Add a one-line note in the Python subsection about the packageManager option being locked to 'uv' and kept only for compatibility, so readers do not spend time researching a non-choice.

The comparison table in 'When to use' uses two columns with resource types in rows — the orientation is correct but the 'Build handled by' row label reads oddly when the table has packaging modes as columns. Consider swapping to packaging modes as rows for standard comparison-table convention.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-grounded against the provided source files. All language defaults, supported version ranges, property names, and descriptions closely track the `StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps`, `StpBuildpackSharedProps`, and language-specific config interfaces in `deployment-artifacts.d.ts`. Cross-links are valid, heading hierarchy is correct, FAQ has the required 10 questions with good category mix, and code examples use proper CodeBlock format. Only minor issues found — an incomplete workload listing and a documentation-depth disparity.

issues.statement: For container-based workloads (web services, worker services, multi-container workloads, batch jobs), see container packaging instead.
issues.reason: The listing of container-based workloads omits private services. The navigation index includes /resources/compute/private-service, and the section-level instructions say container packaging applies to web-service, private-service, worker-service, multi-container-workload, batch-job. The draft's own 'When to use' table and 'When NOT to use' section both fail to mention private services, which could mislead readers into thinking private services use Lambda packaging.
issues.suggestedFix: Add 'private services' to the list: 'For container-based workloads (web services, private services, worker services, multi-container workloads, batch jobs), see container packaging instead.' and link /resources/compute/private-service.

evidence.quote:
type ContainerWorkloadContainerPackaging =
  | StpBuildpackCwImagePackaging
  | ExternalBuildpackCwImagePackaging
  | NixpacksCwImagePackaging
  | CustomDockerfileCwImagePackaging
  | PrebuiltCwImagePackaging;

issues.statement: The 'Other languages' section treats Go, Ruby, PHP, and .NET in a single combined section with one bullet each and no code examples.
issues.reason: Per the per-variant equal depth rule, when a section covers multiple variants each should get roughly equivalent treatment including a working code example. Node.js, Python, and Java each get full CodeBlock examples with surrounding prose explaining property values, while Go/Ruby/PHP/.NET receive only bullet-point summaries. The link to /packaging/function/language-specific-config partially mitigates this, but readers on this page still lack a copyable starting point for these four languages. Go is a special case (zero config), but Ruby, PHP, and .NET each have tunable properties (rubyVersion, phpVersion, dotnetVersion/projectFile) that are set in code examples for the other languages.
issues.suggestedFix: Add at least one combined CodeBlock example showing a non-trivial language (e.g., .NET with dotnetVersion and projectFile set), or individual minimal CodeBlock examples for Ruby, PHP, and .NET. Go can remain prose-only since GoLanguageSpecificConfig is empty.

issues.statement: The 'Excluding files' and 'Excluding dependencies' subsections consist of a single paragraph each with no code example.
issues.reason: The 'Including additional files' subsection gets a full CodeBlock example and prose, while the peer 'Excluding files' and 'Excluding dependencies' subsections are prose-only. Per the section-depth rule, each subsection introducing a configuration option should include at least an example. These properties (excludeFiles, excludeDependencies) are glob/string arrays that benefit from a concrete example showing common patterns (e.g., excluding test fixtures, excluding AWS SDK from the package).
issues.suggestedFix: Add a brief CodeBlock example to either the excludeFiles or excludeDependencies subsection (or a combined example showing both) to match the depth of the includeFiles subsection.

positiveFindings: All language-specific defaults match source @default annotations exactly: Node.js 18, Python 3.9, Java 11, Ruby 3.3, PHP 8.3, .NET 8, outputModuleFormat 'cjs', minify true.
Supported version ranges accurately reflect source union types — including the correct omission of Python 3.10 from the SupportedPythonVersion union.

Property names and descriptions closely track JSDoc in StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps, StpBuildpackSharedProps, EsLanguageSpecificConfig, PyLanguageSpecificConfig, JavaLanguageSpecificConfig, and other interfaces.
All cross-links are valid routes from the navigation index — 10 internal links checked, all present.
Build caching claim ('caches deployment packages based on a checksum, so unchanged code is not re-packaged') directly matches the StpBuildpackLambdaPackaging JSDoc verbatim.
Heading hierarchy is correct: '## When to use' and '## When NOT to use' are peer H2 sections.
ApiReference definitionName='StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps' matches the source interface name exactly.
Code examples use correct CodeBlock intellisense format with valid class names (StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging, LambdaFunction), proper focus markers, and the exact 'TypeScript' tab label.
FAQ has exactly 10 questions with a good category mix: Stacktape-specific (6), AWS service (2), choosing/comparison (2).
The page correctly scopes itself to LambdaFunction resources only, as required by the section instructions for function packaging pages.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the provided packaging types, but it contains several Stacktape-specific claims that are either unsupported by the supplied source files or too broad for what the types actually document. The highest-impact problems are around unsupported default/behavior claims, source-map/log behavior, architecture behavior, and Lambda package-size guidance that reads as Stacktape-specific.

issues.statement: “The `handlerFunction` property overrides which exported function AWS Lambda invokes. This is optional — the buildpack uses the conventional default for your language.”
issues.reason: The source only defines `handlerFunction` as an optional property and describes what it names. It does not document any per-language conventional default or default handler selection behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to: “`handlerFunction` names the function to execute when the Lambda is invoked. Omit it when the default handler behavior is sufficient for your project; set it when your entry file exports a differently named handler.” Avoid naming a specific default unless another source provides it.

issues.statement: “By default, the buildpack includes only what your entry file transitively imports.”
issues.reason: The source documents `includeFiles` and `excludeFiles`, and says JS/TS is bundled from the entry file, but it does not define the default inclusion algorithm as “only transitive imports.” This is especially broad across all supported languages, not just JS/TS.
issues.suggestedFix: Scope the claim to what the source supports: “For JS/TS, Stacktape bundles code starting from the entry file. Use `includeFiles` for runtime files that are not pulled in by the bundler, and `excludeFiles` to remove matched files from the deployment package.”

evidence.quote: For JS/TS: code is bundled into a single file. Dependencies with native binaries are installed separately.
A glob pattern of files to explicitly include in the deployment package.

issues.statement: “Smaller Lambda deployment packages generally lead to faster cold starts.” and FAQ: “Keeping packages under 5-10 MB noticeably reduces cold start time for latency-sensitive functions.”
issues.reason: The general relationship between package size and cold start can be AWS background knowledge, but the specific 5-10 MB threshold is not in the provided source and is presented as actionable tuning guidance. The source only says `disableSourceMaps`, `minify`, and file/dependency exclusions can reduce package size.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the 5-10 MB threshold. Keep the general guidance: “Smaller packages can reduce download/extraction work during cold starts, so exclude files and dependencies you do not need at runtime.”

evidence.quote: Skip generating source maps. Reduces package size but makes production errors harder to debug.
Minify Python code to reduce package size. Makes production stack traces harder to read.

issues.statement: “When you choose `arm64`, the buildpack automatically builds for the ARM architecture — no packaging-level changes are needed.”
issues.reason: The provided source includes an internal `dockerBuildOutputArchitecture` input for packaging and a `DockerBuildOutputArchitecture` union, but it does not document LambdaFunction `architecture` or state that `StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging` automatically builds for ARM based on that resource property.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the section focused on the source-grounded separation: “Architecture is configured on the Lambda function resource, not in `StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging`.” Remove the automatic ARM build behavior unless LambdaFunction source files are provided.

evidence.quote: dockerBuildOutputArchitecture?: DockerBuildOutputArchitecture;
type DockerBuildOutputArchitecture = 'linux/amd64' | 'linux/arm64'; // default for linux

issues.statement: “Use `'*'` to exclude all dependencies from the bundle.” and “Use `'*'` to exclude all non-bundled dependencies.”
issues.reason: The source JSDoc explicitly says `Use *`, without quotes. The property type is `string[]`, so the likely config value is an array containing the string `'*'`, but the draft’s wording could be read as passing the literal string directly rather than an array element.
issues.suggestedFix: Clarify the value shape: “Set the array to `['*']` to exclude all dependencies from the bundle” and “Set the array to `['*']` to exclude all non-bundled dependencies.”

evidence.quote: dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle?: string[];
Use `*` to exclude all dependencies from the bundle.
dependenciesToExcludeFromDeploymentPackage?: string[];

issues.statement: “The buildpack generates source maps by default for JavaScript and TypeScript, so CloudWatch stack traces map back to your original source files.”
issues.reason: The source says JS/TS source maps are automatically generated and separately says local source-map output means CloudWatch stack traces will not be mapped. It does not explicitly state that the default generated source maps make CloudWatch stack traces map back to original source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the narrower sourced wording: “For JS/TS, source maps are generated automatically. If you set `outputSourceMapsTo`, source maps are saved locally instead of uploaded, and CloudWatch stack traces will not be mapped.”

evidence.quote: For JS/TS, your code is bundled into a single file. Source maps are automatically generated.

Save source maps to a local directory instead of uploading them to AWS.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Useful for uploading to external error tracking (Sentry, Datadog, etc.). CloudWatch stack traces won't be mapped.

issues.statement: “For Java, point to your main handler class file. For Go, point to `main.go` or the file containing your handler function.”
issues.reason: The source defines `entryfilePath` generically and gives JS/TS and Python notes, but it does not document Java or Go entry file conventions. This is probably reasonable product knowledge, but it is not grounded in the supplied source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Write cautiously: “For Java and Go, point `entryfilePath` at the source file your project uses as the Lambda entry point.” Or move detailed Java/Go conventions to the language-specific page if that page has supporting sources.

issues.statement: “For rapid iteration without full redeployment, use dev mode (`stacktape dev`), which deploys code changes in seconds and streams logs locally.”
issues.reason: The provided source files are packaging types only. They do not document dev mode behavior, code-change timing, or local log streaming. The link route exists, but the behavioral claim is not source-grounded here.
issues.suggestedFix: Either remove the timing/log-streaming claim or make it a simple cross-link: “For local iteration, see [dev mode](/local-development/dev-mode-overview).”

positiveFindings: The page correctly uses `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[{ label: 'TypeScript', ... }]} />` for Stacktape configuration examples and does not hand-author YAML.
The function packaging split is correctly scoped to `LambdaFunction` resources, and the page links container workloads to the container buildpack page.
The supported language list and documented version unions match the supplied `deployment-artifacts.d.ts` types.
The API reference uses the exact sourced definition name `StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps`.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the primary `StpBuildpackLambdaPackagingProps` surface well: `entryfilePath`, `handlerFunction`, file include/exclude controls, dependency exclusion, and language-specific configuration are all documented with demonstrative examples. I found a few API-completeness and example-scope issues where the page either omits a relevant option while discussing its group, overstates a packaging behavior not grounded in the provided types, or includes non-packaging tuning without enough source support.

issues.statement: The Python section says the key options are `pythonVersion`, `packageManagerFile`, and `minify`, but omits `packageManager`.
issues.reason: The source exposes `packageManager?: SupportedPythonPackageManager` on `PyLanguageSpecificConfig`. Its JSDoc says Stacktape uses `uv` and the option is compatibility-only, but because the section explicitly lists Python key options and says dependency files are installed using `uv`, readers may not know that `packageManager` exists and must be `uv` if provided.
issues.suggestedFix: Add one concise bullet: `packageManager` is kept for compatibility and, if set, must be `'uv'`. Keep it caveated as a compatibility option rather than a normal tuning knob.

evidence.quote: packageManager?: SupportedPythonPackageManager;
Stacktape uses `uv` for dependency resolution and installation. This option is kept for compatibility and must be set to `uv` if provided.
type SupportedPythonPackageManager = 'uv';

issues.statement: “When you choose `arm64`, the buildpack automatically builds for the ARM architecture — no packaging-level changes are needed.”
issues.reason: The provided source files define packaging props and an internal `dockerBuildOutputArchitecture` input type, but they do not show a LambdaFunction `architecture` property or a documented link between Lambda architecture and Lambda buildpack output. This may be true elsewhere, but it is not grounded in the provided source set for this page.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the section to what the page can support, or move architecture guidance to the Lambda function page. If kept, phrase cautiously: `Lambda architecture is configured on the LambdaFunction resource; see the Lambda function page for architecture behavior.` Avoid claiming automatic ARM build behavior unless the relevant LambdaFunction source is included.

evidence.quote: type DockerBuildOutputArchitecture = 'linux/amd64' | 'linux/arm64'; // default for linux
dockerBuildOutputArchitecture?: DockerBuildOutputArchitecture;

issues.statement: The “Excluding dependencies” section documents `excludeDependencies` but does not distinguish it from the Node-specific `dependenciesToExcludeFromDeploymentPackage`.
issues.reason: The draft separately documents both similarly named controls, but the source puts `excludeDependencies` on shared buildpack props and `dependenciesToExcludeFromDeploymentPackage` inside `EsLanguageSpecificConfig`, where the latter only applies to dependencies that are not statically bundled. Without a short distinction, users configuring Node.js functions can easily choose the wrong property.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a sentence after `excludeDependencies`: `For Node.js/TypeScript bundling, use dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle to mark packages external, and dependenciesToExcludeFromDeploymentPackage only to remove already-external packages from the final package.`

evidence.quote: excludeDependencies?: string[];
This only applies to dependencies that are not statically bundled. To exclude a dependency from the static bundle, use `dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle`.

issues.statement: The FAQ states: “Keeping packages under 5-10 MB noticeably reduces cold start time for latency-sensitive functions.”
issues.reason: The provided source supports package size reduction controls, but it does not provide this 5-10 MB threshold or quantify cold-start impact. The FAQ can use general AWS facts, but this specific numeric recommendation is not grounded in the supplied files and reads as a concrete performance threshold.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the numeric threshold or phrase it generically: `Smaller packages can reduce download and extraction work during cold starts, so exclude files and dependencies that are not needed at runtime.`

evidence.quote: Skip generating source maps. Reduces package size but makes production errors harder to debug.
A glob pattern of files to explicitly exclude from the deployment package.

positiveFindings: The draft uses `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab for Stacktape configuration examples and does not hand-author YAML.
The smallest valid buildpack example appears near the top and directly demonstrates `StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging` with `entryfilePath`.
The function packaging union is acknowledged: `stacktape-lambda-buildpack` is compared with `custom-artifact`, and the page points container workloads to container packaging.
The language-specific configuration union is broadly covered: ES/Node, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, PHP, and .NET are all named.
